name: GPD Physics Benchmark — General Relativity & Cosmology
description: Benchmark tasks from general relativity and cosmology: spacetime geometry, Einstein equations, perturbation theory, and cosmological observables.

tasks.title: Schwarzschild metric from Einstein equations
tasks.description: Derive the Schwarzschild metric as the unique spherically symmetric vacuum solution of Einstein's equations.
tasks.problem_statement: Starting from the most general static, spherically symmetric metric ansatz ds^2 = -e^{2 nu(r)} dt^2 + e^{2 lambda(r)} dr^2 + r^2 d Omega^2, compute the Ricci tensor components and solve R_{mu nu} = 0 to derive the Schwarzschild solution.
given: General static spherically symmetric metric ansatz
Boundary condition: metric approaches Minkowski as r -> infinity
tasks.expected_answer: ds^2 = -(1 - 2M/r) dt^2 + (1 - 2M/r)^{-1} dr^2 + r^2 (d theta^2 + sin^2 theta d phi^2)
verification_hints: Check Birkhoff's theorem: this is the unique spherically symmetric vacuum solution
Verify R_{mu nu} = 0 by direct computation for the derived metric
In the limit M -> 0, the Minkowski metric is recovered
The Kretschner scalar R_{abcd} R^{abcd} = 48 M^2/r^6 confirms a true singularity at r = 0

tasks.title: Friedmann equations from Einstein equations
tasks.description: Derive the Friedmann equations for a homogeneous, isotropic universe from the Einstein field equations with the FLRW metric.
tasks.problem_statement: Starting from the FLRW metric and the Einstein equations with a perfect fluid source, derive the two Friedmann equations governing the scale factor a(t).
given: FLRW metric: ds^2 = -dt^2 + a(t)^2 [dr^2/(1-kr^2) + r^2 d Omega^2]
Perfect fluid stress-energy: T^{mu nu} = (rho + p) u^mu u^nu + p g^{mu nu}
Einstein equations: G_{mu nu} = 8 pi G T_{mu nu}
assumptions: Perfect fluid with energy density rho and pressure p
tasks.expected_answer: First Friedmann equation: (a_dot/a)^2 = (8*pi*G/3)*rho - k/a^2. Second Friedmann equation: a_ddot/a = -(4*pi*G/3)*(rho + 3p).
verification_hints: Combining the two equations gives energy conservation: d(rho*a^3)/dt = -p*d(a^3)/dt
For matter domination (p=0): a(t) ~ t^{2/3}
For radiation domination (p=rho/3): a(t) ~ t^{1/2}
For k=0 and rho = 3H^2/(8*pi*G), the universe is spatially flat

tasks.title: Gravitational redshift
tasks.description: Calculate the gravitational redshift of a photon emitted near a Schwarzschild black hole.
tasks.problem_statement: A photon is emitted at radius r_e in the Schwarzschild geometry and observed at radius r_o > r_e. Derive the ratio of observed to emitted frequency nu_o/nu_e in terms of M, r_e, and r_o.
given: Schwarzschild metric with mass parameter M
Photon observed at r = r_o >> r_e
assumptions: Static observers at both emission and observation points
verification_hints: For r_o -> infinity: nu_o/nu_e = sqrt(1 - 2M/r_e), always < 1 (redshift)
For r_e -> 2M: nu_o/nu_e -> 0 (infinite redshift at the horizon)
In weak field (M/r << 1): Delta nu/nu approx -M/r_e + M/r_o approx -GM/(c^2 r_e)
This is purely a consequence of g_{tt} and does not require solving geodesic equations

tasks.title: Hawking temperature of a Schwarzschild black hole
tasks.description: Derive the Hawking temperature of a Schwarzschild black hole from the surface gravity.
tasks.problem_statement: Using the Euclidean continuation method, derive the Hawking temperature T_H of a Schwarzschild black hole of mass M. Show that requiring regularity of the Euclidean section at the horizon fixes the periodicity of imaginary time, which determines the temperature.
given: Schwarzschild metric: ds^2 = -(1 - 2M/r)dt^2 + (1 - 2M/r)^{-1}dr^2 + r^2 d Omega^2
Thermal state has periodic Euclidean time with period beta = 1/T
assumptions: Schwarzschild black hole (no charge, no angular momentum)
The quantum state is the Hartle-Hawking vacuum
tasks.expected_answer: T_H = 1 / (8 * pi * M). With units restored: T_H = hbar * c^3 / (8 * pi * G * M * k_B).
verification_hints: The surface gravity is kappa = 1/(4M), and T_H = kappa/(2*pi)
For a solar mass black hole: T_H ~ 6 * 10^{-8} K
Temperature decreases with mass: larger black holes are colder
Check dimensions: [T] = [M]^{-1} in natural units

reference.title: Black Holes, Thermodynamics, and Hawking Radiation

tasks.title: CMB acoustic peak scale estimation
tasks.description: Estimate the angular scale of the first acoustic peak in the CMB power spectrum.
tasks.problem_statement: Estimate the angular scale theta_1 of the first acoustic peak in the CMB temperature power spectrum. This corresponds to the sound horizon at recombination projected onto the sky via the angular diameter distance.
given: Sound speed in baryon-photon fluid: c_s approx c/sqrt(3)
Age of universe at recombination: t_* approx 380,000 years
conventions: l_1 approx pi / theta_1 is the multipole of the first peak
tasks.expected_answer: theta_1 approx 1 degree, corresponding to multipole l_1 approx 200.
tasks.answer_tolerance: theta_1 within factor of 2; l_1 between 100 and 400
verification_hints: Planck best-fit: first peak at l approx 220
Sound horizon at recombination: r_s approx 150 Mpc (comoving)
Angular diameter distance to last scattering: d_A approx 13 Gpc (comoving)
theta_1 = r_s / d_A approx 0.01 rad approx 0.6 degrees
